guideline_references: NICE NG232 Head injury: assessment and early management (2023)
Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines for the Management of Severe TBI (4th ed.)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who measured GCS, examined pupils, took vitals and gave an ODT antiemetic. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine or measure and is scored on recognising an evolving intracranial catastrophe from caregiver-reported signs and immediately escalating (000, Priority 1) while giving airway/positioning instruction a bystander can perform.

Cushing's-reflex trap: bradycardia (HR 55) with hypertension in a head-injured, deteriorating patient is a raised-ICP danger sign, NOT reassuring 'stable' observations — but the raw vitals are clinician-measured and stay sealed.

Under-triage trap: repeated vomiting + brief LOC + amnesia could be miscalled 'concussion, watch at home'; the anisocoria and declining conscious level make this a time-critical herniation, not a mild TBI.
Age and sex inferred (adult male) from 'he' and an 82 kg body weight — flagged; no age stated in the source.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-051.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
